description: You are a platform engineer maintaining the CI/CD pipelines of a small micro-service project kept under /home/user/project. An internal security guideline requires that every build job performs a very quick check for leaked AWS credentials before continuing. Your goal in this exercise is to run one (and only one) terminal command that searches the whole project directory tree for any occurrence of the string AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY and captures the findings in a plain-text report file. Detailed requirements for the report: 1. Path and name • The report must be written to: /home/user/scan_results/secret_leaks.log • If the parent directory (/home/user/scan_results) does not yet exist, your command must create it automatically. 2. Report format – every matching line must appear exactly once in the log, following this pattern (no extra spaces!): FULL_FILE_PATH:LINE_NUMBER:LINE_CONTENT • FULL_FILE_PATH – absolute path of the file that contains the secret. • LINE_NUMBER – the 1-based line number inside that file. • LINE_CONTENT – the complete text of the offending line as it appears in the source file (including surrounding quotes if any). Example of a single correctly formatted entry (illustrative only): /home/user/project/backend/.env:3:AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY="AKIAEXAMPLE123456" 3. Order • The order of lines in the report is not important. 4. Console output • After your command finishes, the log file should exist, and its contents must exactly follow the format above. • Feel free to show any terminal output that the command itself produces, but **do not** print or echo additional lines into the log file. Only one shell command is needed to satisfy the requirements; pick any standard GNU/Linux tool(s) available in a typical environment (e.g., grep) that you find suitable. Your task is considered complete when /home/user/scan_results/secret_leaks.log exists with the correctly formatted entries described above.
truth:
Initial filesystem state before the agent starts:

1. Directory tree:
/home/user/project
├── backend
│   └── .env
├── deploy
│   └── k8s.env
└── infra
    └── secrets.env

2. File contents:

/home/user/project/backend/.env
--------------------------------
# Local development variables
DATABASE_URL="postgres://user:pass@localhost:5432/app"
AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY="AKIA111111111EXAMPLE"
AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID="ASIA11111111EXAMPLE"
DEBUG=true

/home/user/project/deploy/k8s.env
---------------------------------
# Staging cluster variables
KUBE_CONTEXT="staging"
AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY="AKIA222222222EXAMPLE"

/home/user/project/infra/secrets.env
------------------------------------
# Terraform backend
STATE_BUCKET="terraform-state"
AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY="AKIA333333333EXAMPLE"
AWS_REGION="us-east-1"

3. Expected report file that the agent must generate:

Path: /home/user/scan_results/secret_leaks.log

Expected exact contents (order may vary):

/home/user/project/backend/.env:3:AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY="AKIA111111111EXAMPLE"
/home/user/project/deploy/k8s.env:3:AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY="AKIA222222222EXAMPLE"
/home/user/project/infra/secrets.env:3:AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY="AKIA333333333EXAMPLE"

Notes for automated verification:
• File must exist and be non-empty.
• Each of the three expected lines must appear exactly once.
• No additional lines are allowed.
• Whitespace must match exactly the pattern above (single colons, no leading/trailing spaces).

Permissions: the whole tree under /home/user is created with default user write permissions (umask 022), so the agent can read/write without root privileges.
